Name: PRISMA 2020
LongName: Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses 2020 Statement
Description: PRISMA 2020 provides an updated set of evidence-based reporting items for systematic reviews and meta-analyses. It aims to ensure transparent, complete, and reproducible reporting of the rationale, methods, results, and interpretation of systematic reviews.
Aim: To improve the transparency, completeness, and reproducibility of reporting in systematic reviews and meta-analyses, enabling readers to assess methods, risk of bias, certainty of evidence, and applicability of findings.

Scope.Coverage: Completed systematic reviews with or without meta-analysis evaluating health-related interventions, exposures, diagnostics, prognostics, or other clinical and public health questions.
Scope.Focus: Comprehensive reporting of review rationale, objectives, eligibility criteria, information sources, search strategies, study selection, data collection, risk of bias assessment, synthesis methods, results, certainty of evidence, and funding.
Scope.ContrastWith: Not for primary studies such as randomized trials (use CONSORT), observational studies (use STROBE), or trial protocols (use SPIRIT). Not for scoping reviews unless adapted PRISMA-ScR is explicitly used.

primary.description: Page MJ, McKenzie JE, Bossuyt PM, et al. The PRISMA 2020 statement: an updated guideline for reporting systematic reviews. BMJ. 2021;372:n71.

items.item_name: Identify the report as a systematic review
items.item_question_background: Clear identification ensures the article is indexed and retrieved appropriately and distinguishes it from narrative reviews or primary studies.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Check whether the title explicitly identifies the report as a systematic review (and/or meta-analysis).

items.item_question_background: Abstracts are often the only section read; incomplete summaries can mislead decision-making and evidence synthesis.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Verify that the abstract follows PRISMA 2020 for Abstracts guidance, including objectives, eligibility criteria, information sources, risk of bias methods, synthesis methods, included studies, results, limitations, interpretation, funding, and registration.

items.item_question_background: A clearly articulated rationale situates the review within existing knowledge and explains why the review was needed.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract statements describing the rationale for the review in the context of existing evidence.

items.item_question_background: Explicit objectives define the review question and prevent post hoc framing of findings.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract explicit statement(s) of the review objective(s) or research question(s), including components such as participants, interventions/exposures, comparators, and outcomes where applicable.

items.item_question_background: Eligibility criteria determine which studies contribute evidence; unclear criteria can introduce selection bias and reduce reproducibility.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract inclusion and exclusion criteria, including study characteristics (design, participants, interventions, comparators, outcomes) and report characteristics (years, language, publication status).

items.item_question_background: Transparent reporting of information sources allows assessment of comprehensiveness and risk of missing relevant studies.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract all information sources searched (databases, registers, websites, organisations, reference lists) and the date each source was last searched.

items.item_question_background: Full search strategies enable replication and appraisal of sensitivity and specificity of the evidence search.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract full search strategies for each database and information source, including all search terms, limits, and filters used.

items.item_question_background: Selection methods (e.g., number of reviewers, independence) affect risk of selection bias and reproducibility.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract details of the study selection process, including how many reviewers screened records and reports, whether independently, and any automation tools used.

items.item_question_background: Data extraction methods influence accuracy and completeness; unclear processes increase risk of errors.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract details on how data were collected from reports, number of reviewers involved, independence, methods for obtaining or confirming data, and any automation tools used.

items.item_question_background: Prespecifying outcomes reduces selective reporting and clarifies which results are central to the review question.
items.item_extraction_instruction: List and define all outcomes for which data were sought, including whether all compatible results were collected and any prioritisation rules.

items.item_question_background: Collecting and reporting key study characteristics enables assessment of heterogeneity and applicability.
items.item_extraction_instruction: List and define all other variables for which data were sought (e.g., participant characteristics, intervention details, funding sources) and any assumptions made about missing or unclear information.

items.item_question_background: Risk of bias assessments underpin interpretation of findings; without them, readers cannot judge internal validity.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract methods used to assess risk of bias in included studies, including tools used, number of reviewers, independence, and any automation.

items.item_question_background: Choice of effect measure influences comparability and interpretation across studies.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Specify effect measures used for each outcome (e.g., risk ratio, odds ratio, mean difference).

items.item_name: Synthesis methods – process for deciding study eligibility for synthesis
items.item_question_background: Transparency about synthesis eligibility avoids post hoc grouping and selective combination of studies.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Describe how studies were deemed eligible for each synthesis and any grouping decisions.

items.item_question_background: Data preparation steps (e.g., conversions, handling missing statistics) affect pooled estimates and reproducibility.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Describe methods used to prepare data for presentation or synthesis, including handling of missing summary statistics or data conversions.

items.item_name: Synthesis methods – tabulation and visual display
items.item_question_background: Clear tabulation and visualization ensure transparent presentation of individual study results.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Describe methods used to tabulate or visually display results of individual studies and syntheses (e.g., forest plots, tables).

items.item_name: Synthesis methods – model and heterogeneity assessment
items.item_question_background: Model choice and heterogeneity assessment affect pooled estimates and confidence in findings.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Describe synthesis methods, including model(s) used (fixed/random effects), methods to identify statistical heterogeneity, and software used.

items.item_question_background: Exploring heterogeneity helps explain variability in results and assess robustness.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Describe methods used to explore possible causes of heterogeneity (e.g., subgroup analysis, meta-regression).

items.item_question_background: Sensitivity analyses test robustness of findings to analytic decisions or assumptions.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Describe sensitivity analyses conducted to assess robustness of synthesized results.

items.item_question_background: Assessment of reporting biases (e.g., publication bias) is essential to evaluate completeness of evidence.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Describe methods used to assess risk of bias due to missing results (e.g., publication bias, selective reporting).

items.item_question_background: Certainty (or confidence) in the body of evidence determines strength of conclusions and recommendations.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Describe methods used to assess certainty/confidence in the body of evidence (e.g., GRADE).

items.item_question_background: Transparent reporting of selection results allows readers to understand how many records were screened and included, and why others were excluded.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract numbers of records identified, screened, assessed for eligibility, and included, ideally with a flow diagram.

items.item_question_background: Listing excluded studies with reasons enhances transparency and reduces suspicion of selective inclusion.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract citations of excluded studies that appeared to meet inclusion criteria and reasons for exclusion.

items.item_question_background: Study characteristics provide context for interpreting heterogeneity and applicability.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract characteristics of included studies (e.g., design, participants, interventions, comparators, outcomes, funding).

items.item_question_background: Presenting risk of bias judgments enables interpretation of individual study credibility.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract risk of bias assessments for each included study.

items.item_question_background: Transparent presentation of individual study results supports verification and secondary analyses.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract summary statistics and effect estimates for each study and outcome, ideally with structured tables or plots.

items.item_name: Results of syntheses – characteristics and risk of bias
items.item_question_background: Contextualizing synthesis with study characteristics and risk of bias informs interpretation of pooled results.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Summarize characteristics and risk of bias among studies contributing to each synthesis.

items.item_question_background: Providing pooled estimates with measures of precision and heterogeneity is essential for interpretation.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract results of all statistical syntheses conducted, including summary estimates, precision (e.g., 95% CI), and heterogeneity measures.

items.item_question_background: Reporting heterogeneity investigations clarifies sources of variability and robustness of conclusions.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract results of subgroup analyses or meta-regression exploring heterogeneity.

items.item_question_background: Sensitivity analyses demonstrate robustness of results to alternative assumptions or methods.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract results of sensitivity analyses conducted.

items.item_question_background: Results of reporting bias assessments inform the likelihood that findings are influenced by missing or selectively reported studies.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract results of reporting bias assessments (e.g., funnel plot asymmetry, statistical tests).

items.item_question_background: Presenting certainty ratings clarifies how much confidence decision-makers should place in the evidence.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract certainty/confidence ratings for each key outcome and summary of findings.

items.item_question_background: Interpreting results in context of other evidence prevents overstatement and clarifies contribution.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract interpretation of results in the context of existing evidence.

items.item_question_background: Discussing limitations of included studies informs risk of bias and applicability.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract discussion of limitations of the evidence included in the review.

items.item_question_background: Reflecting on review process limitations promotes transparency about potential methodological weaknesses.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract discussion of limitations of the review methods used.

items.item_question_background: Implications guide future research and practice based on the strength of evidence.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract implications for practice, policy, and future research.

items.item_question_background: Registration and protocol availability deter selective reporting and allow verification of prespecified methods.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract registration number, registry name, protocol availability, and details of amendments.

items.item_question_background: Sources of support can influence review conduct and interpretation; transparency allows assessment of conflicts.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract sources of financial and non-financial support and role of funders.

items.item_question_background: Competing interests may bias conduct or interpretation; disclosure supports trust and appraisal.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract declared competing interests for review authors.

items.item_name: Availability of data, code, and other materials
items.item_question_background: Availability statements enable verification, replication, and reuse of review data and analytic code.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract statements about availability of data, analytic code, and other materials used in the review.

Aliases: Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses
EvidenceExamplesPositive: This systematic review and meta-analysis was conducted in accordance with PRISMA 2020.
We followed PRISMA guidelines and present a PRISMA flow diagram of study selection.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: We conducted a randomized controlled trial and report according to CONSORT.
This observational cohort study is reported according to STROBE.
This clinical practice guideline was developed and reported with AGREE II.
Matching.Explain: Matches primary reports of completed systematic reviews and meta-analyses that explicitly frame review methods or PRISMA reporting; excludes guideline-development manuscripts, protocols, and primary studies.
